text: The surgeon frees the splenocolic and phrenocolic ligaments, preparing to dissect the short gastric vessels. The hilum is then approached for dissection, typically using a vascular stapler for ligation.

The surgeon demonstrates the separable cartridge mechanism, showing how to open and lock the cartridge with a simple press. The cartridge is blue, and the face jaw has a groove for bending. Gore-Tex tape is applied on either side of the jaws for use in partial splenectomy. The instrument is articulated, allowing it to bend up to sixty degrees and return to a straight position.

The surgeon uses a tripolar device to perform a splenectomy, holding and coagulating the short gastric vessels before cutting them with the inbuilt blade. The tripolar is attached to a force triad high-frequency electrosurgical machine, facilitating precise coagulation and cutting.

The surgeon separates the esplenocolic and frenicocolic ligaments from the spleen using a tripolar instrument, which functions as a blunt and sharp dissector, as well as a bipolar coagulator. Traction is applied to form a clear dissection plane, while the Maryland retractor prevents accidental piercing of the sling. Care is taken to avoid collateral damage to the splenic flexure of the colon and the lower pole of the spleen.
The surgeon carefully skeletonizes the splenic artery and vein, preparing them for sealing using energy sources. This meticulous process requires patience to ensure complete isolation of the vessels.

The surgeon identifies the tail of the pancreas near the surgical field and highlights the importance of careful dissection around the short gastric vessels and the greater curvature of the stomach. The hilum is visible, and the surrounding fat is being skeletonized to ensure safe stapling, especially when using an endogia stapler.

The surgeon separates the remaining fibers of the lower pole of the spleen from the colon, allowing the spleen to be lifted and freed. There is a minor burn on the peritoneum of the abdominal wall due to overshooting with energy devices, which is safe compared to potential bowel perforation. The pancreas is visible in the surgical field.

The pancreas is visible and must be handled carefully. The lower pole of the spleen is completely free, allowing the hilum to be pulled over the surface. The surgeon begins to skeletonize the hilum, with the artery positioned posteriorly and the vein anteriorly, ensuring precise dissection near the hilum.

The surgeon removes peri-hilar fat to create a window between the splenic artery and vein. The lower pole of the spleen is lifted and mobilized. The surgeon coagulates at two places before cutting between them, using intermittent activation of the device, ensuring not to press for more than three seconds at a time. The artery and vein are clearly visible.

The surgeon identifies the splenic artery and vein, creating a window between them. Using bipolar harmonic, the artery is coagulated at two places before cutting in between. The blade is visible as it cuts through the coagulated section.

The surgeon uses a blade to coagulate and cut the vein at two places, demonstrating the use of energy for splenectomy. The pulsating splenic artery is visible, successfully coagulated with energy, though this technique is generally avoided due to risks. The hilum is completed, and the remaining short gastric vessels are cut using an inferior and anterior approach.

The surgeon addresses the remaining short gastric vessels during the splenectomy procedure. The lateral approach is mentioned as an alternative technique, which involves complete mobilization of the spleen before addressing the hilum.
